summary: Gruber analyzes Volkswagen CEO Herbert Diess's remarks about not being afraid of Apple entering the car market, arguing that unlike Ed Colligan's famously clueless dismissal of the iPhone in 2006, Diess's comments are actually reasonable. Apple hasn't demonstrated any competence in building cars themselves, only in interfaces and software. However, Gruber identifies a subtle flaw in Diess's thinking: the assumption that tech companies take over markets overnight. Apple's history shows the opposite — they enter markets slowly and then quietly redefine them over years, which is the pattern Volkswagen should actually worry about.
key_points: Diess's remarks are not an 'Ed Colligan moment' — Apple has shown nothing suggesting car-building competence, unlike the iPod's clear foreshadowing of iPhone capabilities
No market Apple has entered was taken over overnight: iPod took two years as Mac-only, iPhone took three years to outsell iPod, Apple Watch was written off after two years
The revealing flaw in Diess's thinking is his belief that tech sectors get taken over 'at a single stroke' — that's not how any of Apple's market entries have worked
Even in a best-case scenario, an Apple car would take many years to reshape the auto industry because car replacement cycles are much longer than phone cycles
Apple's pattern is to enter a market looking unimpressive by existing definitions, then quietly redefine the market itself
money_quotes: I'd like to think that no one has made more hay over Ed Colligan's infamous 'PC guys are not going to just figure this out' quote — just a few weeks before the unveiling of the iPhone — than yours truly.
Apple hasn't shown anything that suggests they'll be good at designing and producing cars. The dashboard interface? Sure. But the car part of the car? Nothing Apple has ever done is like that.
I mean, what do you want him to say? That Volkswagen executives are soiling their lederhosen at the thought that Apple might enter the car market?
No market gets taken over overnight. The iPod spent its first two years as a Mac-only peripheral with a FireWire port.

There is no such thing as a 'typical tech-sector that you could take over at a single stroke'. That Diess seems to think that's how any of the markets where Apple currently competes work — that's the thing that would worry me if I were at Volkswagen.
Apple doesn't do overnight. They walk into your market, and a few years in you realize they've quietly redefined your market and now you're years behind.
Apple enters the market and all that the established entrants see is how Apple's new product doesn't measure up to the market's current definition.
key_insight: The danger Apple poses to incumbents isn't overnight disruption but patient, years-long market redefinition — and the real warning sign is when a CEO dismisses a threat based on the false premise that tech takeovers happen quickly.
